coredata.dc:title: One lead to numerous: A DNA concatemer-based fluorescence aptasensor for selective and sensitive Acinetobacter baumannii detection
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Limin
Tan, Jinshan
Zhang, Cai
Ding, Xiaoteng
Wu, Tianming
Shi, Yanfeng
Chen, Tao
Huang, Chao
Qu, Yue
Zhao, Zhen
Xu, Yuanhong
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and precise analysis of Acinetobacter baumannii plays a crucial role in inhibition of healthcare-associated infectious diseases and development of optimal treatment strategies. Previously reported fluorescence sensors have been promising, but were hindered by poor selectivity and low sensitivity due to the complex detection environment and limited identification ability. Thus, specifically recognised and effectively amplified strategies are vital for overcoming the shortcomings of existing analytical methods. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence aptasensor was constructed for the quantification of A. baumannii based on aptamer-mediated DNA concatemers. The self-assembled DNA concatemers were anchored on the surface of micromagnetic beads by aptamers as an intermediation embellished with numerous carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Effective recognition and capture of A. baumannii cells by the aptamer immediately triggered the release of DNA concatemers. Meanwhile, a magnified fluorescent signal wherein a single A. baumannii cell results in numerous CQDs would be generated. The as-prepared biosensor exhibited an excellent detection and quantification limit (LOD) as 52.67 (11.20) cells/mL, LOQ = 155.3 (10.80) cells/mL over a wide linear range (9.80×102 to 1.25×105 cells/mL) for A. baumannii analysis, which was superior to previously reported methods. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited satisfactory accuracy and good reproducibility in urine samples obtained from patients with bladder cancer and healthy individuals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA concatemer
Fluorescence
Aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of micro magnetic beads with carboxyl groups
Culture and detection of A. Baumannii
Preparation and characterisation of the CQDs and MMBs
Clinical feasibility of the fluorescence aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs of (A) CQDs and (B) MMB. (C) CIE chromaticity diagram of CQDs. (D) FTIR spectra of CQDs and MMB.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, SA-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) and SA. (B) Excitation and emission spectra of CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) before and after streptavidin modification. (C) Native PAGE result of the chain reaction of aptamer-DNA concatemers line 1 (aptamer 2 μM); line 2 (aptamer 2 μM, primer 1 2 μM); line 3 (aptamer 2 μM, primer 2 2 μM); line 4 (Aptamer 2 μM, primer 1 2 μM and primer 2 2 μM); line 5 (primer 1 2 μM); line 6 (primer 2 2 μM); line 7 (primer 1 2 μM, primer 2 2 μM); line 8 (2 μM aptamer, 2 μM primer 1 and 2 combined to the MMB) line 9 (DNA marker). (D) The excition and emission spectra of MMB after modification with aptamer, primers and CQDs. (E) The hydration particle size of MMB, aptamer anchored MMB, primers elongated aptamer anchored MMB, and MAC. (F) The zeta potential of MMB and MAC.

Condition optimization of (A) temperature, (B) incubation time, (C) concentration of MAC and (D) concentration of aptamer. Concentrations of the A. baumannii are 3×104 cells/mL. Error bars mean standard deviation (n = 3).

(A) The sensitivity of MAC to A. baumannii. (B) Linear relationship of MAC to A. baumannii. (F value = 11031, p less than 0.05 by the one sample t-test) (C) Selectivity of MAC to A. baumannii, concentrations of the A. baumannii are 2×105 cells/mL (compared with 2×105 cells/mL E. coli and S. aureus). (D) Stability of the MAC. Error bars mean standard deviation (n = 3).

Illustration of the principle of DNA concatemer based fluorescence aptasensor for A. baumannii. detection. (A)The reaction of aptamer and DNA concatemers, (n) means numerous cycles. (B) The construction of the DNA concatemer based fluorescence aptasensor. (C)The analysis of A. baumannii.
Comparison of different methods of A. baumannii detection.

head.ce:title: One lead to numerous: A DNA concatemer-based fluorescence aptasensor for selective and sensitive Acinetobacter baumannii detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Urology, Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases
The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Urology, Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China (Y. Xu).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L. Li and J. Tan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aptamer mediated DNA concatemer was successfully anchored on the surface of magnetic beads and embellished with numerous CQDs, the recognition of A. baumannii would lead to separation and generating an ‘one A. baumannii to numerous CQDs’ signal amplification method, thus fulfilling the precise and rapid A. baumannii analysis.

Rapid and precise analysis of Acinetobacter baumannii plays a crucial role in inhibition of healthcare-associated infectious diseases and development of optimal treatment strategies. Previously reported fluorescence sensors have been promising, but were hindered by poor selectivity and low sensitivity due to the complex detection environment and limited identification ability. Thus, specifically recognised and effectively amplified strategies are vital for overcoming the shortcomings of existing analytical methods. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence aptasensor was constructed for the quantification of A. baumannii based on aptamer-mediated DNA concatemers. The self-assembled DNA concatemers were anchored on the surface of micromagnetic beads by aptamers as an intermediation embellished with numerous carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Effective recognition and capture of A. baumannii cells by the aptamer immediately triggered the release of DNA concatemers. Meanwhile, a magnified fluorescent signal wherein a single A. baumannii cell results in numerous CQDs would be generated. The as-prepared biosensor exhibited an excellent detection and quantification limit (LOD) as 52.67 (11.20) cells/mL, LOQ = 155.3 (10.80) cells/mL over a wide linear range (9.80×102 to 1.25×105 cells/mL) for A. baumannii analysis, which was superior to previously reported methods. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited satisfactory accuracy and good reproducibility in urine samples obtained from patients with bladder cancer and healthy individuals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Acinetobacter baumannii is a non-motile, pleomorphic, and aerobic gram-negative opportunist pathogen [1–3] that spreads through human contact and airborne contamination by adhering to medical equipment or clothing [4,5]. It can cause acute infections, especially in intensive care unit patients, leading to vascular diseases, urinary tract infections, meningitis, and pneumonia [6–10]. Thus, there is an urgent need to limit its spread and design an analytical method for rapid identification in suspected samples. To date, various methods have been developed to quantify A. baumannii, including bacterial culture methods [11], mass spectrometry [12,13], immunoassays (e.g. enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays) [14], polymerase chain reactions (PCR) [15,16], and electrochemical [17] and fluorescent methods [16]. In particular, fluorescence methodologies provide a well-developed platform for the detection of A. baumannii, owing to their simple operation, cost-effectiveness, and rapid response. However, the low sensitivity and poor selectivity have hindered the application of most reported fluorescence methods [18–20]. Therefore, the development of a simple and reliable method with high selectivity and sensitivity for A. baumannii detection is desirable.

Aptamers have been explored to improve the selectivity of A. baumannii analytical methods owing to advantages, such as easy modification, high stability, low cost, and high target selectivity and affinity. However, low sensitivity remains a problem for aptamer sensors because of low target efficiency. To overcome this challenge, DNA concatemers, which are duplexes formed by the hybridisation chain reaction of auxiliary DNA and signal probes [21], have been developed as direct signal amplification strategies for analysing small molecules [22], proteins [23], nucleic acids [24], and cancer cells [25]. In addition, aptamer-mediated DNA concatemers (aptamer-DNA concatemers) have shown great potential for A. baumannii detection as specific recognition and signal amplification units.

Herein, an ‘one lead to numerous’ fluorescence aptasensor strategy was used to analyse A. baumannii based on magnetic beads and aptamer-DNA concatemers (MACs). This device was constructed by integrating micro magnetic bead (MMB), aptamers, DNA concatemers and carbon quantum dots (CQDs; Scheme 1 ). The aptamers served as intermediates linking the MMBs and DNA molecules, and the conformational transition was triggered by aptamer-A. baumannii interaction to release DNA concatemers. A long DNA concatemer was formed via DNA self-assembly and binds to numerous CQDs through strong biotin-streptavidin interactions. CQD-embellished DNA concatemers can magnify the analytical signal of aptamer-captured A. baumannii, producing a magnification effect from one A. baumannii to numerous CQDs. The prepared fluorescence aptasensor showed a low limit of detection as 52.67 (11.20) cells/mL and high sensitivity. Furthermore, the as-designed sensor can be used to analyse human urine samples, with recoveries ranging from 93 % to 106 %, indicating good reliability and repeatability.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an FS5-TCSPC-1189–0317-A3732-1 spectrophotometer. UV-vis absorbance spectra were recorded using a Mapada UV-6300 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi XPS (Al Kα X-ray) instrument. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope (200 kV). The zeta potential and hydrodynamic size of the as-prepared products were characterised using a Zetasizer Nano ZS 90. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer. Flow cytometric analysis was performed using a Nano FCM (U-30, Nano Fcm, Xiamen Fuliu Technology Co., Ltd., China).

O-Phenylenediamine (OPD), p-terephthalic acid (p-TA), FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, H2SO4, NH3·H2O, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), KMnO4, and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Tryptone, NaCl, soy peptone, streptavidin (SA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), ethanol, HEPES, and MgCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. A. baumannii was obtained from Green Valley Co. Ltd., China. Apt-Aci49, Primer 1, and Primer 2 were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. All DNA oligonucleotides were dissolved in Tris-EDTA buffer to prepare stock solutions (100 μM) and the sequences are listed in Table S1. Urine samples were obtained from the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of micro magnetic beads with carboxyl groups
ce:section.ce:para: MMBs were synthesised by modifying a previous method [26]. Briefly, 8.1 g FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in 150 mL deionised water and heated to 70 °C under conditions of gentle shaking. Next, 4.6148 g FeCl2·4H2O and 18 mL 25 wt% NH3·H2O were added immediately, followed by 1 mL oleic acid was slowly added for 1 h. Subsequently, the resulting precipitate was acquired by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with ethanol and deionised water. After centrifugation, the precipitate was dissolved in 0.1 μM H2SO4 and 62.5 μM KMnO4, and then diluted to 200 mL with deionised water and ultrasonic reacted at 40 Hz for 4 h. The sediment was collected and washed three times with ethanol and deionised water 15 times at 5000 rpm for 5 min each cycle until the supernatant became clear and was subsequently freeze-dried. Finally, MMBs with carboxyl groups were obtained after abrasion and filtration through a 300 mesh sieve.

CQDs were prepared via one-step solvent heating with melioration based on a previously described method [27]. Briefly, 1.5 g OPD and 0.75 g p-terephthalic acid were added to a 5 mL beaker and heated to 120 °C for 5 min on a heating plate. Then, the as-obtained solid was dissolved in 20 mL deionised water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min as the precipitate turned light green. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm), purified using 50 % ethanol, and dried by vacuum freezing for future use.

First, the CQD carboxyl groups were activated using EDC and NHS, and 2 mg of CQDs was dissolved in 10 mL of pH 5.50 borate buffer solution (0.2 M H3BO3, 0.05 M Na2B4O7 .10 H2O), followed by 8 mg EDC, and 16 mg NHS, while the pH was regulated to approximately 8.0, and incubated for 20 min to obtain carboxyl-activated CQDs. Next, streptavidin (0.1 mg) was added to 1 mL carboxyl-activated CQDs and incubated for 2 h at 25 ℃. This was followed by the addition of 10 μL of 5 wt% BSA for 10 min. Then, the as-obtained mixture was washed three times with borate buffer (pH 7.0) and centrifuged (5 min, 7000 rpm) in an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (100 K) to obtain the streptavidin-modified CQDs.

The preparation of the aptasensor is shown in Scheme 1 B. First, the MMB carboxyl group was activated by EDC-NHS and 1 mg MMB was added to 800 μL of 5 mg/mL EDC-NHS PBS (pH 7.20–7.40), and shaken gently for 10 min at 25 °C. Second, 1 mg carboxyl-activated MMB was mixed with 2 μM aptamer in reconstituted 1 mL HEPES binding buffer (5 mM MgCl2, 0.1 M NaCl, 1 mM HEPES) under gently shaking conditions for 4 h at 25 °C for aptamer integration. The aptamer-modified MMB (MMB-Apt) was obtained by centrifugation (5 min, 3000 rpm). Third, 10 μM primer 1 and primer 2 were added to 1 mg aptamer-modified MMB and gently shaken at 25 °C for 1 h. DNA concatemer-modified MMB (MMB-Apt-primer 1, 2) was constructed via centrifugation (5 min, 3000 rpm). Finally, 20 μL streptavidin-modified CQDs was added to the mixture and reacted at 25 °C for 1 h. MACs were obtained by centrifugation (5 min, 3000 rpm) and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture and detection of A. Baumannii
ce:section.ce:para: A. baumannii was cultured in sterile nutrient agar medium (15 g tryptone, 5 g NaCl, and 5 g soy peptone dissolved in 1 L deionised water) [28] at 150 rpm shaking for 10 h at 35 °C in a table concentrator. For A. baumannii analysis, 1 mL A. baumannii culture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 2 min and redispersed in deionised water, and the concentration of the bacterial solution was measured by Nano FCM. After serial dilution, the A. baumannii solution was incubated with 0.20 mg/mL MACs for 35 min at 30 °C. After centrifugation for 5 min at 3000 rpm, the sediment was MMB anchored with A. baumannii using an aptamer, and the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant containing CQD-embellished DNA concatemers was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer. In the artificial spike sample analysis, urine samples from controls and bladder cancer patients were diluted 10 times and spiked with A. baumannii for analysis using the newly developed method.

Urine samples (samples 1 and 2 are urine samples from healthy donors, samples 3–5 are urine samples from bladder cancer patients) were collected from the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University. The five real samples were analysed using the constructed sensor and culture counting methods. The samples were spread onto A. baumannii-selective media (15 g tryptone, 5 g NaCl, 5 g soy peptone 1.5 g agar, 0.5 mg polymyxin B, 10 mg rifampicin, 10 mg trimethoprim, and 10 mg amphotericin B dissolved in 1 L deionised water [46]), incubated for 10 h at 35 °C and counted by gel imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterisation of the CQDs and MMBs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-synthesised CQDs was analysed by TEM (Fig. 1 A), and found to exhibit a symmetrical size with a diameter of approximately 5.66 (0.650) nm, as calculated by counting the CQDs (100 particles) in the TEM images (Fig. S1). A 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 1C. The colour coordinates of the CQDs (activated at 280 nm) were × = 0.156 and y = 0.0437, obtained using the emission spectra and chromaticity calculator. The CIE chromaticity coordinates for the CQDs are shown in the blue area [29], and three dominant excitonic processes (12.48, 2.39, and 1.41 ns) with nanosecond luminescence lifetimes were observed (Fig. S2A) [30]. The CQD surface functional groups were characterised by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 3300–3470, 1700, and 1224 cm−1 were attributed to OH [31], CO [32], and CO [33] stretching, respectively. These results demonstrate that sufficient activating COOH groups were present on the CQD surface. Furthermore, the composition of the CQDs was analysed using XPS. As shown in Fig. S2B, three major peaks attributed to the elemental species C, N, and O were detected. High-resolution XPS spectra of C and O (Fig. S2C and D) for CQDs were analysed; the results showed a peak at 288.6 eV corresponding to the CO signal in the C1s spectrum [33,35], while peaks at 532.6 eV originating from CO [34] and 533.3 eV from CO [36] were observed in the O1s spectrum, consistent with the FTIR results.

TEM was used to analyse the morphology of the as-synthesised MMB. As shown in Fig. 1B, MMBs aggregated into uniform small clusters with a diameter of approximately 8.65 (0.240) nm (Fig. S3A), as calculated by counting the MMBs (50 particles). The FTIR and XPS were used to analyse the MMB chemical structure and functional groups. The FTIR peaks at 3300 and 1600 cm−1 were attributed to OH and CO stretching vibrations (Fig. 1D), respectively [37]. The results indicated the presence of carboxyl groups. As shown in Fig. S3B, three major peaks attributed to the elemental species of C, O, and Fe were clearly observed. High-resolution XPS spectra of C (Fig. S3C) of the MMB samples showed peaks at 289.6 and 284.2 eV attributed to CO and CC, respectively. In addition, as shown in the XPS spectra of the O 1 s core-level peaks (Fig. S3D), the peak at 532.6 eV originates from the O of OC and those at 530.4 and 529.6 eV were ascribed to Fe3O4 and FeO(OH), respectively. The peaks at 710.2 and 725.2 eV in the high-resolution XPS of Fe were attributed to Fe3+ 2p 3/2 and Fe3+ 2p 1/2, respectively, while the double peaks at 717.7 and 713.1 eV were attributed to Fe (II) [38,39] (Fig. S3E). These results confirm that carboxyl-group-modified MMB was successfully constructed.

CQDs with or without streptavidin modification were analysed by fluorescence and UV-vis absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 A, changes in the absorption peaks (CQD peak blue-shifted from 450 to 280 nm and streptavidin blue-shifted from 320 to 280 nm) indicate that streptavidin was successfully modified on the CQD surfaces through amidation [37]. From Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity of streptavidin-modified CQDs significantly increased compared to that of the bare CQDs, further proving that streptavidin was successfully modified. Changes in the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential further confirmed the successful preparation of streptavidin-modified CQDs (Fig. S4A–C). To confirm the self-assembly of DNA concatemers, agarose gel electrophoresis was used to analyse the products [40]. From lane 4 in Fig. 2C, long DNA concatemers were fabricated by consecutive DNA hybridisation of primers 1 and 2. The long DNA concatemers were successfully self-assembled on the MMB-Apt due to hybridisation between primer 1 and the aptamer. Furthermore, a clear increase in fluorescence intensity was observed with aptamer and primer modification of the MMBs. However, the embellishment of streptavidin-modified CQDs resulted in fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2D) [41], indicating that MMB fluorescence was quenched as the CQDs were embellished. The strong and specific interaction between streptavidin and biotin allowed the biotin-modified DNA concatemers to combine with numerous streptavidin-modified CQDs to form MAC, further increasing the hydrodynamic diameter (Fig. 2E). In addition, the zeta potential change (Fig. 2F) and increase in hydrodynamic size was confirmed by Nanoflow experiments. To investigate the importance of the fluorescence signal output of the MMBs, biosensors were constructed with and without MMB modification. After incubation with A. baumannii, fluorescence with and without MMB was clearly distinguished; the results showed that the output fluorescence signal was dramatically impaired in the absence of the aptamer-modified MMB conjugated to A. baumannii (Fig. S6).

As illustrated in Scheme 1C, quantification of A. baumannii was achieved by incubation and separation with MACs. The supernatant fluorescence intensity was measured after the separation. To obtain excellent analytical performance, the experimental conditions (MAC concentration, aptamer temperature, and incubation time) were investigated. To obtain the strongest fluorescence, the incubation time and temperature were optimised, with the most remarkable variations observed at 35 min and 30 °C (Fig. 3 A and B). Owing to the separation caused by the aptamer-modified MMBs, an appropriate concentration of MACs provided better performance for A. baumannii analysis. The results showed that 0.2 mg/mL MAC exhibited the best fluorescence intensity. Similarly, a reasonable amount of aptamer is vital for amplification because the aptamer concentration directly influences the amount of DNA concatemers and 0.25–5 μM aptamer was selected for optimisation studies. When 2 μM aptamer was used, a wide range and good linear relationship were observed. The following experimental conditions yielded optimal results: 2 μM aptamer, 0.2 mg/mL MAC, 30 °C incubation temperature, and 35 min incubation time.

Under optimal conditions, the proposed MAC was incubated with various concentrations of A. baumannii. As shown in Fig. 4 A, fluorescence intensity was enhanced with increasing A. baumannii concentration. The fluorescence intensity changes ((I-I0)/I0) increased linearly with increasing A. baumannii concentrations (from 9.80×102 to 1.25×105 cells/mL), where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities without or with A. baumannii, respectively. The linear regression equation was fitted as y = 3.096x + 0.812 (R2 = 0.997; Fig. 4B), and the F value (11031) was higher than the tabulated F value (5625) upon using the one sample t-test result indicating that p was less than 0.05, confirming that the regression equation was statistically significant [43,44]. The detection limit (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were estimated using the formula LOD n = 3.3(SENn −2σx 2 + h0SENn −2 σx 2 + h0σycal 2)1/2 and LOQ n = 10(SENn −2σx 2 + h0SENn −2 σx 2 + h0σycal 2)1/2 [43]. The LOD of A. baumannii was estimated as 52.67 (11.20) cells/mL, lower than that obtained using previous analytical methods (Table 1 ). The fluorescence aptasensor also exhibited a low LOQ, estimated as 155.3 (10.80) cells/mL, much lower than that found in normal human urine (103 cell/mL) [42]. The sensitivity and analytical sensitivity of the fluorescence aptasensor were analysed using the following equations: SENn = σx/σy and γn = SENn/σx [43,45], respectively, exhibiting high sensitivity of 3.10 (0.160) and analytical sensitivity of 167 (0.860).

To evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescence aptasensor for A. baumannii, a specificity assay was performed with tenfold excess Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus under the same conditions. In Fig. 4C and Fig. S7, the changes in the fluorescence intensity of A. baumannii were significantly larger than those observed with excess E. coli and S. aureus. These results demonstrate that the fluorescence aptasensor exhibits high selectivity for A. baumannii detection. To verify MAC stability, A. baumannii was incubated with the biosensor for different durations under the optimised conditions, showing that the MAC was stable for 15 days and retained good analytical performance for 1 month (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clinical feasibility of the fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To further test its applicability, the MAC was used in 10 times diluted human urine samples (samples 1–3 are bladder cancer patients, 4–6 are healthy subjects, tested samples analysed in triplicate) to assess practicality. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries ranged from 93.0 % (2.60 %) to 106 % (4.90 %), demonstrating good accuracy. Good reproducibility of the proposed fluorescence aptasensor was displayed, as the RSD was ≤ 0.20 % for the six tested concentrations. The t-test was applied to estimate the credibility of the recovery [45], and the experimental and critical t values (Table S2) showed samples 1–4 were not significantly different, while 5 and 6 were significantly different. To further test the applicability, the fluorescence aptasensor was used to detect A. baumannii in human urine samples (samples 1 and 2 from healthy subjects, samples 3–5 from bladder cancer patients). The results are presented in Fig. S8 and summarised in Table S3. The detection results showed no A. baumannii in the collected samples, consistent with the culture counting method results. These results further demonstrate that the fluorescence aptasensor can be used to analyse human urine samples.

In summary, this work presents a novel and effective ‘one lead to numerous’ fluorescence aptasensor based on MACs for determination of A. baumannii with high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, A. baumannii was successfully detected in diluted urine samples of bladder cancer patients from a clinical laboratory and healthy individuals, showing satisfactory accuracy and good reproducibility. Table 1 provides a comparison of this newly developed analytical method with other A. baumannii detection techniques, showing better sensitivity and accuracy for A. baumannii compared to the literature methods. Thus, this the strategy has broad prospects for biochemical analysis and clinical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874079), Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2021JQ04), Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (No. tsqn201909088), Outstanding Youth Innovation Team of Universities in Shandong Province (2019KJA027)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108297.
